metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cleanup instruction to the skill (e.g., `git worktree remove <path>` or `git worktree prune`) to be run on skill completion, failure, or abandonment, so the created worktree does not leak.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new git worktree (filesystem state) but specifies no RETIRE/cleanup path. Git worktrees do not auto-remove; each skill invocation or abandoned run will leave behind a worktree directory and metadata entry that accumulates indefinitely.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / What section — the `git worktree add ... origin/main` instruction
findings.suggested_fix: Append a teardown step to the skill's Phase 2 (and any early-exit path): `git worktree remove --force <worktree-path> && git worktree prune`. Document this as mandatory regardless of success or failure outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a teardown instruction to the new setup directive specifying when and how to remove the worktree (e.g., `git worktree remove <path> && git branch -d <branch>` after the session concludes or the skill finishes reporting).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a persistent filesystem artifact (a git worktree) but specifies no lifecycle / cleanup instruction. Git worktrees are not auto-removed; every skill invocation accumulates a worktree directory and a registered worktree entry in `.git/worktrees/`. The plan's scope section ('Docs/skill content only') and ACs are entirely silent on teardown.
findings.location: Scope section and Acceptance Criteria (all four AC lines)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a default worktree path/name in the plan (e.g. `git worktree add ../rebar-debug-work origin/main`) so the concrete mechanism is fully specified and reproducible.
findings.finding: The `git worktree add ... origin/main` command uses an ellipsis for the worktree directory path, leaving the executor to choose an arbitrary name/location. The AC says the instruction must name the 'concrete mechanism' but the concrete path argument is unspecified, making the instruction not fully reproducible as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The instruction names the concrete mechanism (`git worktree add ... origin/main`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `git worktree add ... origin/main` with a concrete example such as `git worktree add ../rebar-debug-work origin/main` in both the plan text and the AC, or add a sentence stating 'use a temporary path such as `../rebar-debug-work`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for each of ACs 1–3 (e.g. `grep -n 'git worktree add' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` for AC2, and a structural check that the instruction appears before the first Phase heading for AC1).
findings.finding: No proving command is given for ACs 1–3. The plan states what SKILL.md must contain but provides no grep or diff command an executor could run to verify each criterion is satisfied, leaving done-ness unverifiable without interpretation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Verification' section with: (1) `grep -n 'git worktree add.*origin/main' examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md` to prove AC2; (2) a check that the matched line number precedes the first Phase 1/Phase 2 heading to prove AC1's 'at the start' placement; (3) `git diff origin/main -- examples/claude-skills/rebar-debug/SKILL.md | grep -v 'worktree'` review to prove AC3 (no other semantic changes).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the skill instruction how to handle a pre-existing worktree path (e.g., use a timestamped/unique path, or check-and-remove before `git worktree add`) so the setup step is idempotent and does not fail silently on retry.
findings.finding: The plan gives no idempotency or collision-handling guidance for `git worktree add`: if the target path already exists (e.g., from a previous aborted run), the command fails with an error and the skill halts at setup with no recovery path specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'The instruction names the concrete mechanism (`git worktree add ... origin/main`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a unique worktree path per invocation (e.g., `git worktree add /tmp/rebar-debug-$(date +%s) origin/main`) or add a guard: `git worktree remove <path> 2>/dev/null || true` before the `git worktree add` call.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale in the 'Why' or 'What' section explaining that `git worktree add` is the project's established convention for agent isolation (per CLAUDE.md and rebar-implement SKILL.md) and why it fits rebar-debug's context specifically.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Why'/'What' sections identify the problem to solve but do not state a positive rationale for WHY `git worktree add` (rather than a plain branch checkout or other isolation mechanism) is the right fit for this skill — there is no citation to the project's established convention (CLAUDE.md L483-484, rebar-implement SKILL.md Phase 1) that makes this the defensible choice.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why' section, after the anti-pattern sentence, add: 'The project's contributor rules (CLAUDE.md) and the rebar-implement skill already mandate `git worktree add ... origin/main` as the isolation mechanism for agent work; applying the same convention to rebar-debug is the natural fit and keeps all code-writing skills consistent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback/error branch to the worktree-setup instruction specifying what the agent must do if `git worktree add` fails (e.g., surface the error and halt rather than silently continuing in the current checkout).
findings.finding: The plan specifies no fallback when `git worktree add ... origin/main` fails (e.g., path collision on re-invocation, network failure fetching origin, repository state preventing worktree creation). Without a fallback, an executing agent has no protocol guidance and may silently continue in the existing stale checkout — the exact anti-pattern the plan is trying to prevent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 2: 'The instruction names the concrete mechanism (`git worktree add ... origin/main`).'
findings.suggested_fix: In the new setup directive, add: 'If the worktree cannot be created (path collision, fetch failure, or any non-zero exit from `git worktree add`), stop and report the error — do not proceed in the existing checkout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the `git worktree add ... origin/main` placeholder in the instruction to specify a concrete path/branch naming convention (e.g., `../<repo>-debug-<ticket-id>` and `-b debug/<ticket-id>`) consistent with the project standard in CLAUDE.md.
findings.finding: The AC specifies the mechanism as `git worktree add ... origin/main` but leaves both the worktree path and branch name as an unresolved ellipsis. The rebar-implement skill (the direct structural comparator) uses explicit `<path>` and `-b <branch>` placeholders with naming guidance. Without a naming convention, an executor will choose arbitrary names, risking path collisions on repeated invocations of the skill and making worktrees unidentifiable after the fact.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC line 2: 'The instruction names the concrete mechanism (`git worktree add ... origin/main`).'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the project's CLAUDE.md pattern: `git fetch origin && git worktree add ../<repo>-debug-<ticket-id> -b debug/<ticket-id> origin/main`, where `<ticket-id>` is derived from the problem being debugged.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
